Land Rover Range Rover Velar D275 Engine Codes

mainEntity.name: How much does a Range Rover Velar D275 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned Velar D275 (306DT) engine typically costs £3,500–£5,500 supply only. Fitting adds £2,000–£3,000 depending on location and workshop rates. Used engines start from £1,500–£3,500 supply only. Main dealer new engines start from £10,000–£15,000+ supply only.

mainEntity.name: Is the Range Rover Velar D275 engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The D275 uses the 306DT — a 3.0-litre V6 diesel from the AJD-V6 family. It shares the same fundamental design as the higher-output D300. The engine has a mixed reputation — it's smooth and refined, but the crankshaft bearing failure risk is well-documented across the AJD-V6 family. With meticulous maintenance, some owners achieve high mileages, but the risk of sudden failure remains.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Velar D275?

acceptedAnswer.text: The main known issues are: (1) Crankshaft and bearing failure — the most devastating fault, shared with the wider AJD-V6 family, (2) DPF clogging — particularly on vehicles used for short urban journeys, and (3) Low-frequency vibration or resonance at 1,400–1,500 rpm — reported by multiple owners.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Velar D275 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on vehicle value and condition. Against a well-kept £18,000–£30,000 Velar D275, a £4,000–£5,500 recon is 15–30% of the car's value — often worth it given Velar values have remained relatively strong. However, for higher-mileage examples or those with other issues, evaluate total repair costs before committing.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Velar D275 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: The D275 uses the 306DT engine code — a 3.0-litre V6 diesel producing 275 PS and 625 Nm of torque. The engine code is stamped on the cylinder block. Tell us your registration and we'll confirm it for free.

mainEntity.name: Can I prevent crankshaft failure on the D275?

acceptedAnswer.text: Prevention is not guaranteed, but you can significantly reduce the risk: (1) Change oil every 10,000 miles or less with the correct JLR-spec oil, (2) Have injector copper washer seals inspected at 60,000–90,000 miles, (3) Regularly check the dipstick for diesel smell or rising oil level, and (4) Never ignore an oil pressure warning or rhythmic knocking. No amount of maintenance guarantees prevention, but these steps dramatically reduce the risk.

replacementCosts.h2: Land Rover Range Rover Velar D275 Engine Replacement Costs
replacementCosts.figuresNote: Figures above reflect the L560-generation 306DT, the only engine used in the Velar D275. Newer Velar models such as the D300 feature more advanced engines with higher replacement costs.
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting a Velar D275 engine typically takes 14–18 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent).

problems.title: Crankshaft & Bearing Failure (AJD-V6 Family)
problems.symptoms: Rhythmic knocking from lower engine block that worsens under load, oil pressure warning light flickering, sudden engine seizure with no warning
problems.repairCost: £1,500–£3,000 (early intervention — bearing replacement if caught immediately)

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: stop driving immediately if you hear knocking or see the oil pressure warning

problems.recommendation: The 306DT shares the AJD-V6 family's well-documented crankshaft bearing failure risk. The failure sequence is understood: injector copper washer seals harden over time, allowing combustion gases to leak into the rocker cover. This pressurises the crankcase, reduces oil pump efficiency, and starves the bearings of oil. Once the bearings spin or the crankshaft snaps, the engine is beyond economical repair. At the first sign of rhythmic knocking or an oil pressure warning, stop driving and arrange recovery to a specialist. This is the single most important thing to check when buying a Velar D275.

failureLink.label: Read the full crankshaft failure guide → /failures/tdv6-crankshaft-bearing-failure/ [NEW FAILURE PAGE NEEDED]

problems.title: DPF Clogging & Regeneration Issues
problems.symptoms: DPF warning light, "Restricted Performance" message, reduced power, increased fuel consumption
problems.typicalMileage: Any mileage — short urban journeys accelerate the issue
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only (does not progress to engine replacement if addressed)

urgency.label: Check
urgency.text: book inspection urgently if warning appears

problems.recommendation: Velar D275 owners have reported DPF-related issues, including a red warning and severe limp mode after long journeys. Like all modern diesels with DPF systems, the D275 is sensitive to short urban journeys. If you're buying a D275 primarily for city use, factor in occasional longer runs to allow passive DPF regenerations. If the DPF warning appears, book a forced regeneration immediately — ignoring it can lead to expensive DPF replacement.

failureLink.label: Read the full DPF clogging guide

problems.title: Engine Vibration & Resonance (1,400–1,500 rpm)
problems.symptoms: Low-frequency vibration or resonance from the lower body at around 1,400–1,500 rpm, noticeable when accelerating or decelerating through that range
problems.typicalMileage: Can occur from low mileage — reported from 600 miles
problems.repairCost: Varies — may require multiple dealer visits to diagnose

urgency.label: Monitor
urgency.text: book inspection if vibration becomes intrusive

problems.recommendation: Multiple D275 owners have reported a low-frequency vibration or resonance at around 1,400–1,500 rpm. This appears to be a characteristic of some D275 vehicles rather than a mechanical fault, but if it becomes intrusive, book an inspection. Some owners have reported that dealer interventions have not resolved the issue.

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: Velar D275 — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: Viable if caught immediately — but requires specialist assessment
Full engine replacement is the only reliable fix
May be a characteristic rather than a fault

buyingChecks.title: Buying This Velar D275? Check These First
buyIf: * Full service history with 10,000-mile or less oil change intervals
* Evidence of regular motorway use (DPF health)
* No history of oil pressure warnings or rhythmic knocking
* Extended warranty in place — this engine is high-risk
avoidIf: * Any history of oil pressure warnings — this is a critical warning sign
* Rhythmic knocking from the engine — stop and walk away
* DPF warning lights or "Restricted Performance" messages
* No service history — the 306DT is unforgiving of neglect

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The Velar D275 is a rare and short-lived badge — produced only from 2018 to 2020. It uses the 306DT V6 diesel, a detuned version of the D300's engine. While the 306DT is generally refined and smooth, it shares the AJD-V6 family's crankshaft bearing failure risk — the same issue that plagues the Discovery and Range Rover Sport TDV6/SDV6 engines. Buy with full service history and budget for preventative maintenance. Without history? Walk away — the risk of a £5,500+ engine replacement is simply too high.

cta.label: → Compare Velar D275 engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Land Rover Range Rover Velar D275 (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Cold Start — Listen for knocking or rattling that worsens with revs
* Oil Pressure Warning — Has it ever flickered? This is a critical warning sign
* Service History — Including the 306DT engine and confirmed oil change intervals — non-negotiable
* Rhythmic Knocking — Stop immediately if you hear knocking from the lower block
* DPF History — Ask about driving patterns; short urban journeys are a red flag
* Restricted Performance — Any warning messages? This often indicates DPF or turbo issues
* Vibration Check — Test drive through 1,400–1,500 rpm; any low-frequency resonance?
* Warranty Status — Extended warranty is highly recommended
